questions.question: Why does dividing by sqrt(d_k) matter in the attention formula Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V?
options: It normalizes the output vectors to unit length
It prevents dot products from growing large and pushing softmax into regions with vanishing gradients
It reduces the computational complexity from O(n^2) to O(n log n)
It ensures the query and key matrices have the same dimensionality
questions.explanation: Without the scaling factor, large dot products push the softmax into saturated regions where gradients are extremely small, making training unstable. Dividing by sqrt(d_k) keeps the variance of the dot products at roughly 1, keeping the softmax in a useful range.

questions.question: A chatbot maintains a 40-message conversation. The user sends message 41. How does the LLM 'remember' the earlier messages?
options: The model stores a hidden state server-side from the previous call
The client re-sends all 41 messages in a single prompt, and the model processes them in one forward pass
The model retrieves earlier messages from a fine-tuning checkpoint
A separate memory module injects summaries into the model's weights at inference time
questions.explanation: LLMs are stateless — each API call is an independent forward pass with no server-side memory. The client application is responsible for re-sending the full conversation history every time. This is why costs grow with conversation length: you pay for all previous messages on every call.

questions.question:
This function estimates whether a prompt fits in the context window. There's a bug that will cause it to under-count tokens and potentially blow the context limit. Spot it:

```python
def fits_in_context(prompt: str, max_tokens: int = 128000) -> bool:
    # Rough estimate: 1 token ≈ 4 characters
    estimated_tokens = len(prompt) / 4
    return estimated_tokens < max_tokens
```
options: The function should return estimated_tokens <= max_tokens (off-by-one)
Character-based estimation is unreliable — code, non-English text, and whitespace tokenize at very different ratios. Use the actual tokenizer.
The division should be len(prompt) / 3 for a safer estimate
questions.explanation: The '1 token = 4 characters' heuristic breaks down badly for code (where short symbols each become separate tokens), non-English text (which tokenizes 2-3x less efficiently), and whitespace-heavy formatting. The only reliable method is running the actual tokenizer (e.g., tiktoken) against the text. A 'safe' ratio doesn't exist because the variance is too high.

questions.question: You set temperature=0.7 and top_p=0.9 for an API call. The model's forward pass produces logits where one token has 98% probability. How many tokens are in the candidate set for sampling?
options: The full vocabulary (~100k tokens) since temperature > 0
Approximately 70% of the vocabulary due to temperature scaling
Exactly 1 — the 98% token alone exceeds the top_p=0.9 threshold
It depends on top_k, which must also be set
questions.explanation: Top-p (nucleus sampling) selects the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p. If one token holds 98% of the probability mass after temperature scaling, it alone exceeds the 0.9 threshold, so the candidate set contains just that one token. This is why top-p is adaptive — it narrows aggressively when the model is confident and widens when the distribution is flat.
